Then you can derive the truth table from it: - If v ⊨ P (P true) and v ⊭ Q (Q false), then the “if … then …” condition fails, so P→Q is false. - If v ⊭ P, then the condition “if v ⊨ P then v ⊨ Q” holds trivially, so P→Q is true. This is the same vacuity, just expressed without an explicit table.
That semantic clause is exactly the thing I dislike: “if v ⊨ P then v ⊨ Q” is a meta-level conditional. And meta-level “if” is not the same as object-level →. So it’s circular.

It’s not circular because the meta-level “if” is in the metalanguage (English/mathematics) and is not the same symbol as the object-language connective you’re defining. You’re using ordinary reasoning to define a formal semantics. But you’re right that it can feel like it pushes the problem up a level. Still, the meta-level conditional is not being defined by a truth table in the same way; it’s part of the underlying mathematics of the semantics. If you formalize the metalanguage too, you’d need some conditional there as well, but you can treat that as primitive or define it in a different system. At some point, definitions bottom out.
So either way, you can’t avoid vacuity. It’s baked into “if…then…” in mathematics.
It’s baked into classical “if…then…” when interpreted as a universal constraint: “no counterexample.” Vacuity is a standard feature of such constraints.
I want to check my understanding with a puzzle. Evaluate: (P→Q) ∨ (Q→P). Is that always true?

Yes, it’s a tautology in classical logic. Reason: For any truth values of P and Q: - If P is true and Q is false, then Q→P is true (since Q is false). - If P is false and Q is true, then P→Q is true (since P is false). - If both true, both implications true. - If both false, both implications true. So at least one disjunct is always true. More formally: (P→Q) ∨ (Q→P) is the “law of excluded middle” for implication comparability; it corresponds to the fact that material implication defines a total preorder on truth values in a 2-element Boolean algebra.
That’s another weird result. It says any two propositions are comparable by implication in at least one direction. That’s obviously not how real implication works.

Correct: it’s a feature of material implication’s truth-functional nature. It doesn’t mean “P entails Q or Q entails P” in the proof-theoretic sense. It means the formula (P→Q) ∨ (Q→P) is true under every valuation. That’s a purely extensional artifact. This is why material implication is sometimes criticized as not capturing a robust notion of “implies.”
Then why do logicians still call it “implication”?

Historically and practically: because it aligns with many inferential patterns and because in formal proofs, “if … then …” statements are represented by this connective. Also, in Boolean algebra and classical semantics, it behaves like the internalization of entailment: P ⊨ Q iff ⊨ P→Q (when P and Q are formulas and entailment is classical). That is a strong relationship. But yes, the name can mislead beginners.
Wait, P ⊨ Q iff ⊨ P→Q? That seems important. Explain.

In classical propositional logic, semantic entailment P ⊨ Q means: in every valuation, if P is true then Q is true. That is exactly the semantic clause for validity of P→Q: ⊨ P→Q means: in every valuation, P→Q is true; and P→Q is true in a valuation exactly when (if P then Q) holds in that valuation. So: P ⊨ Q  iff  ⊨ (P → Q). This shows why → is called implication: it internalizes the meta-level entailment relation into the object language.
That actually justifies it more than convenience. But it still relies on the same “if P then Q” meta-level condition. Still, okay.
Right, but it shows coherence: the connective is designed so that entailment corresponds to a tautology of that connective.
So if I want an implication connective that internalizes entailment but avoids vacuity, I need a different entailment relation.
Exactly. Change the consequence relation (e.g., relevance entailment, paraconsistent entailment), and the connective that internalizes it will differ.

Let’s do an exercise. Suppose we define a new connective ↣ such that P ↣ Q is false whenever P and Q have no relevant connection. But relevance isn’t truth-functional. So you can’t have a truth table. Is that the point?

Yes. Relevance logics generally make implication non-truth-functional: its truth depends on more than just truth values; it depends on content/structure or on semantics like entailment in relevant models. So if you want to capture “connection,” you typically give up truth-functionality, or you enrich the semantics with additional structure.
Okay. But then why is propositional logic taught first if it’s so misleading about implication?

Because it’s the simplest environment to learn formalization, syntax vs semantics, proof rules, soundness/validity, and it’s computationally tractable. It’s a stepping stone. Many later systems build on it. Also, in many technical domains, truth-functional implication is exactly what is needed: digital circuits, SAT, specification languages, etc.
Specification languages use implication? Doesn’t that cause the same “vacuous truth” bugs?

It can. In formal specification, an implication like “request → grant” is satisfied when there is no request, even if there is never a grant. That can make a spec too weak: a system that never issues requests trivially satisfies it. Engineers handle this by specifying additional properties (like liveness: “eventually request occurs,” or “if request occurs infinitely often then grant occurs,” etc.) using temporal logics (LTL/CTL), which have richer semantics. So yes, vacuity is a known issue; tools even check for vacuous satisfaction.
That’s a good real example. So vacuity is not just philosophical; it affects verification.
Exactly. It’s a feature you must understand to write meaningful specs. Material implication is still the right connective there; you just need to be aware of vacuous satisfaction.

All right. I think I’m finally seeing the shape: material implication is a minimal truth-functional connective that matches entailment and certain proof rules, but it doesn’t capture causation/relevance, and vacuity is a predictable consequence. Still, I want one more thing: explain why “if P then Q” is equivalent to “P is sufficient for Q” but also to “Q is necessary for P.” Those phrases confuse me.

“P is sufficient for Q” means: whenever P holds, Q holds. That is P → Q. “Q is necessary for P” means: P cannot hold without Q. That is also P → Q. So they’re the same relation described from different angles: - Sufficiency: P guarantees Q. - Necessity: Q is required by P. Both correspond to ruling out P ∧ ¬Q.
And “P is necessary for Q” would be Q → P.
Correct. “P is necessary for Q” means: if Q then P. So Q → P.
Okay. Last objection: Why not define implication as “P and Q have the same truth value” or something? That would avoid vacuity maybe.

“Same truth value” is the biconditional P ↔ Q. It doesn’t express implication; it’s stronger and symmetric. It would make modus ponens fail in general: from P and (P ↔ Q) you can infer Q (that part works), but it also forces Q → P, and it doesn’t match conditional proof as used in mathematics. Also, it doesn’t avoid vacuity; it just changes when the connective is true/false.
Fine. I’ll stop fighting it. But I’m still annoyed that the name “implication” suggests more than it is.

That annoyance is common and reasonable. The best way to handle it is to keep a clear separation: - Material implication (→) is a truth-functional connective with that truth table. - Entailment (⊨) and derivability (⊢) are relations between formulas. - Natural-language “if” is not always material implication. Once you keep those separate, the “true when antecedent is false” becomes a manageable technical fact rather than an absurdity.

Evaluate P → Q in each case: 1) P=T, Q=T: T 2) P=T, Q=F: F 3) P=F, Q=T: T 4) P=F, Q=F: T And some compound ones: - ¬P → Q is false only when ¬P is true and Q is false, i.e., when P=F and Q=F. - P → ¬P is false only when P=T and ¬P=F, i.e., when P=T. So P → ¬P is equivalent to ¬P.

Compute: P → ¬P ≡ ¬P ∨ ¬P ≡ ¬P. Yes. It says: “If P then not P” is true exactly when P is false. That matches the constraint view: it forbids P from being true, because if P were true then ¬P would be false and the conditional would fail. So the conditional is equivalent to ¬P.
